replies.content:
1) Yes, and there's another factor. The arrow of cause and effect descends from the spiritual planes to the material plane, and there's a time lag (as experienced from the lower planes where time is fixed and linear). That's the basis of precognition: if you're on the physical plane and can perceive the higher planes, you sense things happening on the higher planes before they happen on the physical plane.

2) Remember that the gods are the basic patterning forces of the cosmos, and when they curse someone, it's not an arbitrary act imposed from outside -- it's as though they said, "Okay, be exactly what you are," and that's what the accursed person does. There's a great story from one of the old Japanese chronicles about an emperor who mocked a god who was speaking through an oracle. The god said in response, "Go in a straight line." When the lights came up, the emperor was stone cold dead. Once the emperor put himself in opposition to what the Japanese call Daishizen, "Great Nature," he was already dead in every sense that mattered, and the god's words simply confirmed that state.

Thus the karma of someone who does horrific things while under a divine curse is the same as that of someone who does the horrific things all by themselves. The god is simply saying, "Become exactly what you are," and the results follow.

comments.title: FHR and the Golden Dawn
comments.content:
Hello! I have two questions, related to the way the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose has been characterized as an offshoot of the Golden Dawn.

1. This one is specific. I'm practicing the Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram and the Middle Pillar exercise (Regardie's version) on a daily basis, as protection and development rites. I also typically perform the Lesser Invoking Ritual of the Pentagram in the morning. Is there a role for the LIRP in the FHR?

2. More generally, are there Golden Dawn practices that should be avoided, or that are actively incompatible with the FHR curriculum? I know that the LBRP and the SoP aren't complementary, working as they do on competing energies. To be honest, I don't do many other GD rituals (the occasional Lesser Ritual of the Hexagram is all, really), although I am still studying the Hebrew alphabet and some of the other material in the early knowledge lectures. Or, put another way, are there GD practices that it would be *good* to include with the FHR work?

I know I've asked after the GD connection before - I have a longstanding affinity for a lot of the GD teachings - and I promise that I won't keep bugging you about it after this!

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: FHR and the Golden Dawn
replies.content:
1) No. The only rituals used in the FHR are the basic daily banishing and energy work -- in your case, the LBRP and the MP -- the rituals of opening and closing a temple, and the rituals of initiation.

2) No. As far as I know, the classic GD rituals aren't harmful and they're not helpful -- they're off on their own, in effect. Don't mix them with the FHR work, but if you want to do classic GD work at other times, that shouldn't be a problem.

replies.title: Re: FHR and the Golden Dawn
replies.content:
I have some piggy back questions on this if I may?

1) If someone is doing LBRP and MP every day - so banishing energy and then drawing it in - is there a purpose to doing LIRP daily as well? In the Golden Dawn Regardie says to do LIRP in the AM and LBRP in the PM but if one has added in MP, is LIRP daily still necessary?

2) So what you’re saying here is I should NOT use the FHR Opening and Closing combined with the consecration ritual for the magic mirror in Circles of Power as I have been contemplating. What problems could be associated with using a mirror for scrying that hasn’t been consecrated? I’m assuming something nasty could potentially come up, but can that be mitigated by just using the mirror while in the Temple after the Opening? Or should I just chill out and wait to do the elemental scryings as they come? I ask because I had wanted to do a scrying mirror as one of my 3 methods of divination with FHR work.

Thank you!
- J

replies.title: Re: FHR and the Golden Dawn
replies.content:
For (1), I think a distinction is drawn between the LIRP and the MP on a couple of fronts. (But I am happy to be corrected!)

First, although both involved balancing energy (within the sphere of sensation), the drawing in and balancing of energies is primarily done both before and after the actual LIRP, by way of the Qabalistic Cross rite. The LIRP is considered a way for beginners to start interacting with non-physical entities under relatively controlled circumstances. One adept specifically pointed out that the use of the pentagrams was intended to "attract and commune with spiritual forces". I think the LRP (both versions) sort of lights you up on the astral plane, as opposed to, say, the LBRH. (By controlled circumstances, I mean with the "guardrails" of the four pentagrams and the protective circle that you are creating.)

I don't think the LIRP's "interactive" intent is a feature/goal of the MP.

So whether or not you use these things may depend on your goals!

replies.title: Re: FHR and the Golden Dawn
replies.content:
1) Nope. The LIRP isn't much used in classic GD work except in the early stages of training; once you add in the MP, there's no need for it, since that's a good solid invocation in its own right.

2) You shouldn't use the Circles of Power consecration, but fortunately you don't have to. The most recent FHR document dump includes a paper on clearing negative energies from an object to make it suitable for divination. Open a temple using the standard ritual, then clear the mirror using any of the methods that appeals to you, then wrap the mirror in silk or linen, and it'll be fine.

replies.title: Re: FHR and the Golden Dawn
replies.content:
1) Thank you very much to you and agitated seraph for your answers to question 1 about LIRP, this is precisely the info I was looking for.

2) This is also perfect, thank you! Am I okay to to use the method of clearing described in that most recent paper even though I’m still on the Candidate Grade? Or should I wait until Sojourner?

Thanks again!

- J

replies.title: Re: FHR and the Golden Dawn
replies.content: 2) You can use it right now. I took that from one of John's tarot sites, where it was posted as a free introductory essay for anyone and everyone to read.

comments.title: Spectral smells?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I was out in our orchard today with my husband. My husband is unusual in not having a scent. Most European-descended males do have a naturally strong scent. I was standing with him when I smelled the scent of a warm man. It was not unpleasant, in fact, I found it attractive, sexy.

I sniffed at my husband's shirt and it was not him. It was not me and it was not our two pet sheep. I smelled it twice, once at the edge of the orchard and again when we were near where we keep the statue of Priapus. He did not smell the man scent.

Our daughter has an exceptionally keen sense of smell and once complained of a spirit in her bedroom here. She said he smelled rank but she could really have been a tracking dog as her sense of smell is off the charts. I think we may have smelled the same scent.

What do you make of this? One other time, I smelled the scent of rose water that I had made for a friend. She sent an owl to visit me and I asked if it was her. I didn't believe it so she sent me the scent of the rosewater I had given her in hospice.

We were walking through the forest today and my husband smelled pipe smoke very clearly. I did not. I loathe tobacco smoke and would have noticed it if it was there.

Also, I pray to the statue of Kwan Yin that my niece gave me. I kiss my fingers and touch her foot. A few times now, I have been filled with tingles after touching her. I also kissed my hand to the moon last night and got a tingle at my heart's centre. This happens now occasionally

Odd but pleasant things.

Maxine

replies.title: Re: Spectral smells?
replies.content: Clairolfaction, as it's sometimes clumsily called, is quite common. William Butler Yeats talks in one of his essays about his experiences with it -- he'd smell the scent of burnt feathers, for example, whenever one of his children was sick. As for kissing the hand, that's a classic way of offering respect to divine beings, so I'm not surprised you're sensing the response!

replies.title: Re: Spectral smells?
replies.content: I always thought Clairolfaction was the occult ability to tell whether someone is really a natural blonde.  😄

comments.title: Keeping your Imagination Wild but Under Control
comments.content:
Good evening!

I hope that everyone in the northern hemisphere is enjoying the transition from Spring into Summer, and that it's less oppressively warm wherever you are than where I am.

First, to share: for any fans of manga, Witch Hat Atelier is a aimed at pre-teen girls, but the art is absolutely lovely, and the premise is basically "magic = art", and it uses that premise to explore what it's like to find your way as an individual creative who also needs to learn the lessons of past masters, using magic as a metaphor. As far as I can tell, it doesn't show much knowledge of real magic, but it has a very deep understanding of creative effort and uses literary fantasy tropes to explore it.

For a question:
1. Some weeks ago in a comment to a previous MM, you told someone not to "domesticate his imagination". In other MMs, though, you've talked about how our imagination is not "only in our head", and that we ought to give some thought to how our mental lives might impact on those around us.

So, my question is, any advice on how to navigate these seemingly disparate goals? How to let our imaginations run wild while also exercising some control?

Thanks very much for this space and all of your effort,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Keeping your Imagination Wild but Under Control
replies.content: That's one of the great questions of human existence, and the only answer I know of is to do your best and learn from your mistakes.

replies.title: Re: Keeping your Imagination Wild but Under Control
replies.content:
Dear Jeff, you asked about aestetics, I wonder (finding the art in the witch hat Atelier also superb, if a bit expensive) if the picture book of Karl Blossfeld(t?) would be of help to you. They are black and white fotos he took of plants for his arts-students. (plants somewhat cut, to be able to fotograph better).
He insisted that the students often don't look close enough while sketching and that working with a deeper study of nature would remedy that. IIRC his works inspired Gaudí and other "Jugendstil"-artists. Plus: he is very certainly dead, so even if the book is almost all pictures it will fit your limitation for this year. ;)
Best wishes, Emily07

replies.title: Re: Keeping your Imagination Wild but Under Control
replies.content: Thanks very much for this, I hadn't heard of Blossfeldt, but his work looks like it would certainly reward close study!

May I ask a question about integrating the Shadow? I am having trouble understanding what this means in practice. I thought that integrating the Shadow archetype means becoming conscious of it.

Through time, I've discovered some aspects of my shadow self. Recently I ended up being smug about someone else's misfortune, which is something I don't like about myself and which previously I wouldn't have been aware of before I learned about my Shadow.

But now that I'm aware of it, I still noticed the feeling of being smug was powerful. So if I push it down, I worry that I am then repressing it. If I let it have sway, then I am allowing a negative tendency to be dominant and give in to it.

I hope this makes sense? I'm just not sure what to do with my Shadow tendencies now that I'm aware of them.

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The crucial step in working with the shadow is learning not to project it onto other people, and that's something you'll have to work with over and over again. Let's take the feeling of smugness. You're feeling superior to the other person, right? That means you're projecting something onto them -- probably your own repressed sense of inferiority. Most smug people have a boatload of insecurities and feelings of inferiority, which they project onto other people so they don't have to face those feelings in themselves.

Any time you feel that someone else is bad or evil or inferior or what have you, you can safely assume that you're projecting something. Look inside yourself to see what it is. As Confucius wrote, "When I see what is virtuous, I commend it. When I see what is not virtuous, I examine my own conduct."

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Now I'm even more glad I asked this question, because your answer shows I hadn't completely understood the concept of the shadow, and my way of thinking about it was a binary: do I repress smugness, or do I go with it and justify it? I knew about projection, and I was aware that my feeling smug was something to do with myself, but my mistake was thinking that this binary was my only option.

What you're suggesting is using any negative trait that I feel about someone else, and use it as a marker that I'm avoiding looking at some important neglected quality within myself. So that would mean integrating the shadow isn't just realizing that I have certain negative qualities, it's becoming conscious about what those negative qualities are masking.

Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a very astute observation!

For whatever it may be worth, the name in its original Greek was spelled ΙΗΣΟΥΣ and pronounced roughly like "ee-ay-SOOS" where the double O has the sound of the double O in English 'food." This Greek name ΙΗΣΟΥΣ was the customary equivalent of the Jewish name Joshua; in the Greek Bible the Hebrew Book of Joshua is called the "Book of Jesus Naue." In Latin the name is spelled IESUS (a 4th-declension noun, so its vocative case is IESU), still pronounced "ee-ay-SOOS."

The initial j-sound in the English version has a complicated back-story of linguistic confusion.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch and Golden Section
replies.content: They're very closely related, and not by accident. One of the things that I've been trying to do over the last couple of decades is to take the system of occult training I inherited from John Gilbert and explore some of the possible variations on it, so that future students of the system have plenty of options to work with. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Food and drugs with magic; Greek SoP
replies.content:
1) Mild doses of caffeine are fine before meditation -- Zen monks are notorious for their fondness for green tea in the breaks between long hours of zazen. Strong caffeine, tobacco, and alcohol should be avoided for an hour or so before meditation. It's also best not to eat right before meditating, since digestion concentrates your blood flow in your stomach and meditation calls it into your brain -- the results of the conflict range from indigestion to falling asleep while meditating.

2) You can certainly use the same deity in the opening and in the six directions -- most people do. Your arrangement of deities seems fine; in your place I'd probably invoke Persephone as spirit below, for reasons of gender balance, but it's your call.

replies.title: Re: SoP Basics

comments.title: A New Ascetic Movement
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Thank you for running these Magic Monday posts. I learn a lot about occult ideas and metaphysics just reading through. I wanted to get your thoughts on something I've been mulling about.

For some time now, I've noticed an interesting set of movements or tendencies that have become popular in the last decade or so: NoFap (abstaining from porn and masturbation), anti-porn, anti-drug, minimalism, and even anti-video game ideas. I noticed among the young men of the now defunct alt-right, they talked about their political conversion in a highly religious way: they gave up porn, drugs etc. and seemed to believe that political activism would lead to inner transformation. Banal realities like tax policy, community organizing, and land management were uninteresting to them, and when they tried to address these issues, the results were always embarrassing and incompetent (That's another story).

To me, it seems that especially among young men we are seeing a desire for a new asceticism, but it's inchoate. It hasn't been able coalesce around any firm set of metaphysical ideas to give it direction and form, but it's there. We've seen ascetic movements become widespread among young men (and eventually young women as well to some degree) during prior civilizational collapses, namely during twilight years of Roman Empire and its Christian monasticism.

Have you observed the same thing and if so, what are your thoughts on it? Do you have any ideas about what a new spiritually ascetic movement in the West or the US might look like?

replies.title: Re: A New Ascetic Movement
replies.content:
There's a fine scholarly book called Subversive Virtue by James Francis. It's about the role of asceticism in the Roman world, and it's highly relevant to the present. The Roman elite classes, like ours, controlled the masses by making them chase after cheap pleasures -- bread and circuses were only part of it -- and so the most radical thing anyone could do was to refuse to be tempted by the goodies. Several Roman emperors exiled Stoics from Rome, because they realized that if a man can control himself, no one else can control him.

The same thing is happening now. We've got a Society of the Spectacle, to borrow a phrase from Guy Debord, which controls people by getting them to wallow in drugs, pornography, mass media, and so on. People who don't want to be dominated by the corrupt society in which we live quite sensibly are drawn to asceticism as a way to freedom. Yes, it's inchoate at the moment, but it's immensely powerful. What needs to happen is that the young men in question need to realize that politics is not spirituality, and joining a political movement will not give them the transcendence they crave. Systematic spiritual practice alone will do that.

Now you know why I put so much time into getting the methods of spiritual practice I know into as many hands as possible...

replies.content: I'm curious if you have any thoughts you'd be willing to share on the idea of fitness and athletics as a spiritual practice?

replies.title: Re: A New Ascetic Movement
replies.content: The history of yoga on the one hand, and martial arts on the other, ought to give you plenty of parallels to reflect on. That is to say, it's a valid approach and can be pursued in any number of ways.

comments.title: The gods looking out for me?
comments.content:
Dear JMG

I have been doing some active trading in the financial markets and while my portfolio is down a lot overall this year, I’ve actually been doing quite well buying and selling in the last few weeks in fairly risky trading (high risks/high rewards). It’s not something I usually do (I’m more of a long term investor) but I’ve been doing surprisingly well for a few weeks.

So of course the markets all tanked very badly from Friday till today after the inflation numbers came out way worse than expected (the stock markets closed over the weekend but many other markets are global and/or trade 24/7).

I had

1. A trade overnight Friday that would lose me a lot of money if the market dropped below a certain price by 9am on Saturday morning. The price held overnight at just a fraction over the danger price, and within 20 minutes of me breathing a sigh of relief at 9am, it started to crash rapidly - if that had happened 20-30 minutes earlier, I would have lost tens of thousands of dollars - but instead I made a small profit.

2. Another trade I entered into on Saturday morning - I thought it was safe at the time, but the market started to go against me and on Saturday night I just had a bad feeling and thought I wanted to get out, which I did and took a small loss (it basically cancelled out my small profit from the morning).

3. Then I entered a third trade on Saturday evening, which I thought was super conservative and that I was very unlikely to lose money (again, I would lose money if the price dropped below a very conservative level by 9am Monday). Throughout Sunday, the price dropped a little bit but was still far away from my danger zone, so I went to sleep fairly relaxed Sunday night (I mean, I didn’t sleep well, with market stress, but I was able to sleep) leaving the trade live.

..Then at 7am on Monday morning, I was woken up by the fire alarm going off loudly - false alarm, but I checked prices before going back to sleep and found that the price had crashed wildly again, right to the border of my danger zone, and quickly exited my trade (again at a small loss). 20 minutes later - crash through my danger zone. If I had slept till my normal time and not been woken up by the fire alarm, I would have been down tens of thousands of dollars.

I guess my question is - is this the kind of thing gods do to help someone, and how can I thank them?

I’m just writing this out to process it for myself and it really feels like someone has been looking out for me, especially the unexpected wakeup call this morning. Plus I’ve been doing surprisingly well in trading in the last few weeks when by rights I should have lost money even before this weekend, in a very difficult market.

I pray every night (to a mix of Hindu gods and a couple that have no name but I’ve kind of been aware of and praying to for many years) but I’m not big on Hindu ritual and going to temples (although I do, once in a long while).

But I specifically didn’t pray for these trades to go well or to be saved from losses in them - just for the general welfare of my family and our finances (which are rather shaky following the recent market carnage).

If this is indeed the kind of thing the gods do, would it be appropriate to just make a visit to the temple and pray there and say thank you? Not sure what else I can do..?

replies.title: Re: The gods looking out for me?
replies.content: It does indeed sound as though someone is looking out for you. The fact that you're praying for your family's welfare and your finances, rather than trying to get something for yourself, is likely to be why they're helping. In your place, yes, I'd make a beeline for the temple and thank them. Someone who knows more about Hindu traditions than I do may be able to suggest other things you can do.

I was reading about vampires in your book on monsters the other day, and I've a thought, a light question, and a heavier question (though I'll understand if the answer to that latter question, if you know it at all, treads too close to forbidden-with-good-reason* knowledge for you to provide it). All of them relate to ancient vampires.

The thought:
While it wasn't universal, there was, as far as I know, a general custom in Kemet of living on the east side of the Nile and building tombs on the west side. And I realized, well, that's a _pretty significant_ bit of running water between most of the living people and most of the not-quite-dead-yet people, and that particular separation might have been a significant reason for the custom. Especially given the Nile's significance in more than just size; I'd not be surprised if revenants with benevolent intentions towards and invitations from the living found their path over the river significantly easier than those with malevolent intentions.

The light question:
I've observed before (though as an outsider, so perhaps there's some explanation an insider would know I've not heard) that pop-culture-vampire fans often seem to associate the ankh with vampirism. This I found pretty odd, given that there are as far as I know (though, again, outsider) not much at all in the way of other connections between vampires and Ancient Egypt. Vampires in evening dress vant to suck blud in Eastern Europe and sparkly vampires in the Pacific Northwest romance teenage girls, but the classic pop culture monster of Egypt is the shambling mummy. Quite a different monster... in pop culture.
Might the pop culture association of vampirism with the ankh be a bit of actual knowledge that somehow got mixed in?

The heavy question:
Did some etheric revenants, when the offerings to them ceased, simply accept their time had come, and let go? I am thinking particularly of those who became revenants not out of personal greed or fear but out of duty to the living, a king who delayed moving on to continue serving the realm, now able to do things he couldn't while living, or, further down the societal ladder, someone staying on as a more active family ancestor, to help protect and guide future generations. Those who spent their lives exploiting others might well choose to simply continue in death, particularly if it offered them a chance to delay for a bit longer facing the karmic consequences of the life they were refusing to leave, and perhaps even those who became revenants with noble motives might end up justifying taking a bit more than they should. Etheric taxation, needed to save the realm. Feeding on someone _else's_ family to keep supporting one's own. But is such degradation _universal_, such that even a revenant with the most noble of starting motives becomes a ravening, murderous parasite if left half-alive long enough, or is enough humanity retained that those who followed the better parts of humanity could keep following those right to the end, by letting that end come?

*I mean, imagine if Jeff Bezos and his ilk got their hands on the secrets, and didn't consider them equally as real as the Dark Lord Voldemort's method of cheating death through murder. Something like this, in the hands of people with vast wealth, apparently pretty much _no_ scruples, care, or empathy where ordinary people are concerned, and a Faustian mindset of infinite expansion? [shivers]
Mind you, I don't expect it would endure for very long, and would end with the vampires _thoroughly_ killed -- but a lot of damage could be done in the meantime.

Second, I'd encourage you to review Israel Regardie's essay The Art of True Healing and begin practicing the expansion of the Middle Pillar exercise he gives there, focusing on Venus/Netzach, which will bring you back more firmly into your body. You're not at any risk of going crazy, but you could have problems with too much spiritual perception too quickly. The changes I've suggested should take care of that, however.

comments.title: Occultism and "magical thinking" OCD
comments.content:
I've had an interest in the occult since childhood, but I haven't made any serious attempts to practice in 20 or so years (I'm 40-ish). Since childhood I've also had "magical thinking" OCD, which consists of intrusive thoughts that I might make something bad happen if I do or don't do some unrelated action. I often have to think specific thoughts to alleviate the anxiety.

This causes me some difficulties with my occult studies and attempts to practice, as you might imagine. A common intrusive thought is that I'm going to cause something to happen by even thinking or reading about a ritual. Of course, even being someone who kinda believes in magic, I know that my intrusive thoughts are irrational, because magic isn't supposed to work that way. But OCD is persistent, and it always asks "But what if you're wrong?"

My question is, what would be your advice for someone like me who wants to dip their toes back into occultism? Is it a bad idea, or could some experience with "the real thing" help alleviate these "monkey's paw" doubts?

replies.title: Re: Occultism and "magical thinking" OCD
replies.content: I don't recommend getting involved in occultism at all until you have your OCD under control. Concentrate on that first.

comments.title: Geomancy and sports results
comments.content:
To the reader last week who was enquiring about geomancy and sports match results:

I've tried to use geomancy to predict the outcome of sports matches, using a "Will X win" or "Will I win if I bet on X" question format. My most reliable answers have come from using the sign in the fifth house - some success but nothing statistically significant so far.

Since a bet on a match is essentially a competition between the better and the bookmaker about who has the better knowledge of the outcome, I don't see gambling on these predictions as unethical.

JMG: is there such a thing as a countermeasure against divination? Given the huge amount of cash floating through the sports betting world, I would expect that someone would try to 'mask' the results from enterprising diviners. Thank you for your time.

comments.title: The Fourth Dimension
comments.content:
Hi Mr. Greer!

Tonight we had a Zoom meeting of my Gurdjieff group. We waxed philosophical and got into the question of the fourth dimension: was it taught as a spiritual dimension in early Christianity, can you observe it in the world around us, is it like Plato’s world of ideas.
I’ve just recently started reading about and practicing the Way of the Golden Section.
My question is, In the WotGS, how should I think about a “higher world”? As a Gurdjieffian, I would think of it as the astral plane. I’ve only gotten a little way into the book. But I plan to continue with the Druidry Handbook next. Maybe you cover higher worlds later on.

Thanks for everything,

E.S.

replies.title: Re: The Fourth Dimension
replies.content:
It depends a lot on what you mean by "a higher world." I'm not that knowledgeable about the Fourth Way, so I don't know the sense that Gurdjieff had in mind when he spoke about the astral plane; in classic occult philosophy, the different planes of being aren't higher worlds, they're different modes of the same cosmic root substance. There are other Cosmic planes, which can be seen as "higher" than ours, but that doesn't relate to the fourth dimension; if you want a sense of the way that occultists see the fourth dimension, this book by Claude Bragdon is a good intro:

https://archive.org/details/aprimerhighersp00braggoog
